title: English Podcast | Talking About Healthy Habits
youtubeTitle: Build Healthy Habits With This Chat
youtubeDescription:
Join Emma and Mike for a calm, natural English conversation about healthy habits — sleep, eating well, exercise, and less screen time. Spoken slowly and clearly for B1-B2 learners, it's an easy way to boost your listening and everyday vocabulary.

🎧 Turn on captions (CC) and just listen.
👉 Subscribe for a new easy English conversation every week!

#LearnEnglish #EnglishPodcast #EnglishListening #B1English #HealthyHabits

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: Hey Mike! It's so good to see you. How have you been?

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Hi Emma! I'm doing okay, but honestly, I'm a bit tired today.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: Oh no, rough night? That sounds like the perfect topic for us, actually.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Right, today we're talking about healthy habits. Let's start with sleep and rest.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: Perfect. So tell me, how many hours of sleep do you usually get?

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: About six hours, I think. I know that's not really enough.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: Yeah, most adults need around seven or eight hours to feel their best.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: That makes sense. I always wake up feeling exhausted instead of rested.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: Do you have any kind of bedtime routine, or do you just crash whenever?

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Not really. I usually scroll on my phone in bed until I fall asleep.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: Ah, that might be the problem. The light from screens can keep your brain awake.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Really? I had no idea it could affect my sleep that much.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: It does. Experts say you should put your phone away about an hour before bed.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: An hour? That sounds hard, but I'm willing to give it a try.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: You could read a book or listen to calm music instead. It really helps.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: What about you, Emma? Do you have a routine that works well?

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: I do! I go to bed at the same time every night, even on weekends.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Wow, that's impressive. I thought weekends were for staying up late.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: They used to be for me too, but a regular schedule changed everything.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: So you wake up feeling refreshed? I can barely open my eyes some mornings.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: Most days, yes. I also avoid coffee after lunch, which makes a big difference.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Hmm, I drink coffee in the evening all the time. Maybe that's why I'm restless.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: Probably! Caffeine can stay in your body for hours, so timing really matters.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Any other tips? I'd love to finally sleep through the night.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: Sure. Keep your room dark and cool, and try not to nap too late.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Those sound doable. I always thought good sleep was just about luck.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: Not at all. With small changes, you can build habits that last for years.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Thanks, Emma. I'm definitely going to fix my bedtime routine this week.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: Great! And good sleep often goes hand in hand with eating well, you know.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Oh, that's a great point. Let's talk about healthy eating next, then.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: Yeah, I really think what we eat matters just as much as how we sleep.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: That's so true. Honestly, my eating habits have been a bit of a mess lately.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: Same here sometimes. Have you been cooking at home or eating out a lot?

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Mostly eating out, to be honest. It's quick, but it's not very healthy.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: I get that. Restaurant food usually has way more salt and oil than we think.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Exactly. When I cook at home, at least I know what's going into my meals.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: That's the big advantage. Plus, you can add more vegetables without even noticing.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Vegetables are my weak point, actually. I never seem to eat enough of them.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: You're not alone there. A simple trick is filling half your plate with veggies.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Half the plate? That sounds like a lot, but it makes sense.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: It's easier than it sounds. I just throw spinach or peppers into almost everything.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Smart. I should start doing that instead of reaching for chips when I'm hungry.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: Ah, snacking. That's where a lot of us slip up during the day.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Definitely me. I snack a lot in the afternoon, usually on junk food.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: Have you tried keeping healthier snacks around, like nuts or fruit?

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: I've thought about it. If they were on my desk, I'd probably grab those instead.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: That's the key, really. We tend to eat whatever is closest and easiest.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: So true. Sometimes I'm not even hungry, I'm just thirsty or bored.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: Oh, that reminds me. A lot of people mistake thirst for hunger, you know.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Really? So drinking more water might actually stop some of my snacking?

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: It can help a lot. I try to drink a glass of water before every meal.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: That's a nice habit. I barely drink any water until the afternoon, sadly.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: Maybe keep a bottle on your desk so you sip throughout the day.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Good idea. Speaking of mornings, do you usually eat breakfast?

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: Always. If I skip it, I feel tired and end up overeating at lunch.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: That happens to me too. I skip breakfast, then eat way too much later.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: A little something in the morning really sets the tone for the whole day.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: You've convinced me. I'll start with oats or eggs instead of nothing.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: Those are perfect choices. Small changes like that really add up over time.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Agreed. Eating better is one step, but I guess moving more is the next big one.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: Right, eating well is only half the story. What about exercise?

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: True. You can eat all the salad you want, but you still need to move your body.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: Exactly. So how do you usually stay active during the week?

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Honestly, I try to walk as much as I can. I get off the bus one stop early.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: That's such a smart trick. Those extra steps really add up over time.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: They do. I read that walking ten thousand steps a day can make a big difference.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: I've heard that too. Do you actually hit that number most days?

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Not always, but my phone reminds me when I'm being lazy. What about you?

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: I go to the gym three times a week. It keeps me feeling strong and energetic.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Three times a week is impressive. What kind of workouts do you do there?

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: Mostly weights and a bit of cardio. I like to mix things up so I don't get bored.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: That makes sense. I always thought the gym was a little intimidating for beginners.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: It can feel that way at first, but everyone is just focused on their own routine.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Good point. The hardest part for me is finding the time after a long day at work.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: I get that. Have you ever thought about doing simple workouts at home instead?

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: A little. What can you actually do at home without any equipment?

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: Plenty. Push-ups, squats, and planks work really well, and they only take ten minutes.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Ten minutes I could manage. I always thought I needed at least an hour.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: That's a common myth. A short workout is much better than nothing at all.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: So if I did ten minutes every morning, would that really be enough?

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: For a start, absolutely. The key is being consistent rather than doing too much at once.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Consistency is exactly where I struggle. I start strong and then give up after a week.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: You're not alone there. Staying motivated is the biggest challenge for most people.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: So what's your secret? How do you keep yourself going week after week?

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: I set small goals and track my progress. Seeing improvement keeps me excited.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: That sounds doable. Do you ever work out with a friend to stay on track?

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: Sometimes. Having a partner really helps because you don't want to let them down.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Maybe we should go for a walk together sometime. That might push me to start.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: I'd love that. Even a relaxed walk counts as moving and staying active.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Then it's a plan. Honestly, talking about this already makes me want to get moving.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: And there's one more thing I've been thinking about lately, my screen time.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Oh, that's a big one for me too. How many hours a day are you on your phone?

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: Honestly? My phone said almost six hours yesterday. I was shocked.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Six hours? That's nearly a whole working day on a tiny screen.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: I know. And most of it was just scrolling without really paying attention.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: That's the tricky part. You pick up the phone for one minute and suddenly half an hour is gone.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: Exactly. So I've started taking small breaks from screens during the day.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: What kind of breaks? Like leaving your phone in another room?

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: Yes, that's one. Every hour I stand up, stretch, and look out the window for a few minutes.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: That sounds simple but really helpful. My eyes get so tired by the evening.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: Mine too. If I stare at a screen all day, I get headaches and I sleep badly.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: I've noticed that as well. Using my phone right before bed always keeps me awake.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: Right, just like we said earlier about sleep. So now I put it away about an hour before bed.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: I should try that. What do you do instead of looking at your phone?

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: I read a book or just chat with my family. It feels much calmer.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: That makes sense. I think we forget how nice it is to relax without any devices.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: Right. Going for a short walk without my phone is one of my favourite ways to switch off.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: I love that idea. No notifications, no messages, just some quiet time.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: Exactly. In the end, it's all about finding balance between work, rest, and fun.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: That's so true. If we work all day and then scroll all night, there's no real rest.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: And screens can be useful too, of course. The key is using them on purpose, not by habit.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Well said. I'm going to set a daily limit and stick to it this week.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: That's a great goal. Even cutting back by one hour a day would help a lot.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Agreed. Small changes really do add up over time.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: They do. Healthy habits aren't about being perfect, they're about little steps every day.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: I couldn't agree more, Emma. This was a really nice chat.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: It was, Mike. Thank you so much for sharing all your ideas today.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: And thank you for listening, everyone. We hope these tips help your daily routine.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: Take care of yourselves, get some rest, and enjoy a little less screen time tonight.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: See you next time. Goodbye, and stay healthy!
